content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

Date : NOV. 10, 1976

To : John K.           Natalie
     Nan              Mickey
     Gina             Dan
     Jay              Bob S.
     Mike             Barbara
     Ken P.           Fred
                      Rich M.

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Ted Prim

Subject: Question from Tony Alperin concerning late
         penalties under Section 91013.

         If a city clerk imposes a late filing penalty pursuant to
Section 91013 on a city official, and the official refuses
to pay the penalty, who is responsible for enforcing the
fine? At the October 5 Opinion Request Meeting, the staff
concluded that either the city clerk or the city's legal
officer (usually the city attorney) is responsible for en-
forcing such fines. These fines are not civil penalties
within the meaning of Section 91001, and are not the
responsibility of the civil prosecutor. (The district at-
torney is the civil prosecutor with respect to most local
candidates). Moreover, such penalties appear to be applicable to the collec-
tion of late filing penalties because that Section
tion of late filing penalties because that section limits
recovery to the "amount or value not properly reported" and
does not limit the recovery of fines. However, pursuant
to Section 91003, a citizen residing in the jurisdiction
could obtain an injunction to require a local candidate
to file his campaign statement, but any late penalties would
be enforceable only by the city clerk or the city's legal
officer.

TP:jo

The image appears to be almost entirely blank or white, with only some very faint marks or artifacts visible at the top and right edges that are not legible text. There is no clear, readable text content to transcribe from this document image.

State of California

Memorandum

To    : John K.        Natalie                     Date  : Nov. 10, 1976
       Nan            Mickey
       Gina           Dan
       Jay            Bob S.
       Mike           Barbara
       Ken F.         Fred
                      Rich M.

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Ted Prim

Subject: Question from Tony Alperin concerning late
          penalties under Section 91013.

          If a city clerk imposes a late filing penalty pursuant to
          Section 91013 on a city official, and the official refuses
          to pay the penalty, who is responsible for enforcing the
          fine? At the October 5 Opinion Request Meeting, the staff
          concluded that either the city clerk or the city's legal
          officer (usually the city attorney) is responsible for en-
          forcing such fines. These fines are not civil penalties
          within the meaning of Section 91001, and thus, are not the
          responsibility of the civil prosecutor. (The district at-
          torney is the civil prosecutor with respect to most local
          candidates). Moreover, the bounty hunter provisions of
          Section 91004 do not appear to be applicable to the collec-
          tion of late filing penalties because that section limits
          recovery to the "amount or value not properly reported" and
          does not include the recovery of fines. However, pursuant
          to Section 91003, a citizen residing in the jurisdiction
          could obtain an injunction to require a local candidate
          to file his campaign statement, but any late penalties would
          be enforceable only by the city clerk or the city's legal
          officer.


TP:jo

[The image appears to be a very faded or low-contrast document where most of the text is illegible or nearly invisible. There are two circular marks at the top of the page, but the body text is too faint to read accurately. Without being able to make out the specific words with confidence, I cannot provide a reliable verbatim transcription as requested.]

embedding.qa_text: **Memorandum** **State of California** **To:** John K., Natalie Nan, Mickey Gina, Dan Jay, Bob S. Mike, Barbara Ken F., Fred Rich M. **Date:** Nov. 10, 1976 **From:** FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Ted Prim **Subject:** Question from Tony Alperin concerning late penalties under Section 91013. If a city clerk imposes a late filing penalty pursuant to Section 91013 on a city official, and the official refuses to pay the penalty, who is responsible for enforcing the fine? At the October 5 Opinion Request Meeting, the staff concluded that either the city clerk or the city's legal officer (usually the city attorney) is responsible for enforcing such fines. These fines are not civil penalties within the meaning of Section 91001, and thus, are not the responsibility of the civil prosecutor. (The district attorney is the civil prosecutor with respect to most local candidates). Moreover, the bounty hunter provisions of Section 91004 do not appear to be applicable to the collection of late filing penalties because that section limits recovery to the "amount or value not properly reported" and does not include the recovery of fines. However, pursuant to Section 91003, a citizen residing in the jurisdiction could obtain an injunction to require a local candidate to file his campaign statement, but any late penalties would be enforceable only by the city clerk or the city's legal officer.
